topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, a glossy snapshot of a sun-drenched coastline, lay crumpled on my desk. My fingers traced the edge of the paper, the texture rough beneath my skin. Liam was moving. Across the country. The words echoed in the silence of my apartment, sharp and unwelcome. I stared at the framed photo of us from that disastrous camping trip last summer, the shared laughter frozen in time. A tightness constricted my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over his contact information, then letting it drop onto the wood. I needed to talk to him, to ask the million questions that felt too enormous to voice. The air in the room seemed thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I managed, the word coming out thin and reedy. The sound of my own voice startled me. Maya had just dropped the news—a confirmation email about her relocation. I stood by the window, watching the familiar street below, the mundane sounds of traffic suddenly feeling alien and distant. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscle vibrating beneath my skin. Every thought cycled through a loop of ‘what ifs’ and ‘how will this change everything?’ A coldness began to seep into my limbs, a creeping numbness that made the warmth of the afternoon sun feel like an accusation. I walked to the kitchen, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles were white, needing something solid to anchor me against the swirling uncertainty.

[story 3]

I reread the text message for the tenth time. It was brief, professional, devoid of the easy familiarity that had defined our friendship for years. Moving. The simple verb carried the weight of a seismic shift. A dull ache settled low in my stomach, a persistent pressure that made focusing on anything else impossible. I paced the length of my living room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. My gaze kept drifting to the empty space on the couch where we used to sprawl out, sharing secrets and terrible movies. I stopped abruptly, running a hand through my hair, feeling a desperate need to smooth down the disarray inside my head. The quiet of the evening amplified the frantic rhythm of my pulse in my ears.
